children.name: Bucorvus
children.description: The largest and heaviest of all hornbills, adapted to a terrestrial lifestyle in the savannas and woodlands of Africa. Unlike arboreal hornbills, ground hornbills have long legs for walking, short toes, and a casque that is open at the top. They are slow to mature and long-lived, with a complex social system centred on cooperative breeding.

children.name: Bucorvus leadbeateri
children.description: The largest hornbill species, standing nearly a metre tall with a wingspan of up to 1.8 metres. It ranges from Kenya and the DRC south to South Africa, inhabiting open savanna and grassland. It has a striking red bare patch of skin on the throat and around the eyes, and lives in family groups of 2 to 11 birds that cooperate to rear young.

children.name: Bucorvus abyssinicus
children.description: Found across the Sahel and savanna belt from Senegal and Mauritania east to Ethiopia and Uganda. It is distinguished from the Southern Ground Hornbill by its blue and red throat pouch and the presence of a conical casque on top of the bill. It is threatened by habitat conversion and persecution, with declining populations across its range.
